metadata.name: WordPress Core Knowledge - HTML API
metadata.description: Tests knowledge of WordPress HTML API (WP_HTML_Tag_Processor, WP_HTML_Processor)

tests.prompt: What does WP_HTML_Processor::serialize_token() return now that the method is public in WordPress 6.9?

choices.text: The raw substring of the original HTML
A normalized, well-formed serialization of the currently matched token
Only the tag name of the current element

tests.explanation: serialize_token() emits a fully normalized, well-formed representation of the current token, useful for safe extraction/rewriting.

tests.prompt: What does WP_HTML_Tag_Processor::set_modifiable_text() do when you try to set script contents that include '<script' or '</script' in WordPress 6.9?

choices.text: Returns false and leaves the SCRIPT tag unchanged
Strips the nested script tags and proceeds

tests.explanation: 6.9 hardens set_modifiable_text() for SCRIPT nodes by rejecting updates containing '<script' or '</script', returning false so callers can abort safely.

tests.prompt: In which WordPress version was WP_HTML_Tag_Processor introduced?
tests.explanation: WP_HTML_Tag_Processor was introduced in WordPress 6.2.0 as a streaming HTML tokenizer that can find tags and modify their attributes without building a DOM tree.

tests.prompt: In which WordPress version was WP_HTML_Processor introduced?
tests.explanation: WP_HTML_Processor was introduced in WordPress 6.4.0 as a tree-aware HTML parser that follows the HTML5 specification's parsing algorithm, building on top of WP_HTML_Tag_Processor.

tests.prompt: What is the relationship between WP_HTML_Processor and WP_HTML_Tag_Processor?

choices.text: WP_HTML_Processor extends WP_HTML_Tag_Processor, adding tree construction
They are unrelated classes with similar APIs

tests.explanation: WP_HTML_Processor extends WP_HTML_Tag_Processor. It inherits all tokenization and attribute modification methods but adds tree construction logic per the WHATWG HTML5 specification.

tests.prompt: What does WP_HTML_Tag_Processor::get_attribute() return for a boolean HTML attribute like 'disabled' in <button disabled>?
tests.explanation: get_attribute() returns the PHP boolean true for boolean/valueless attributes (e.g., <div disabled>). It returns null if the attribute is missing, and the decoded string value for attributes with values.

tests.prompt: What is the maximum number of bookmarks allowed at one time in WP_HTML_Tag_Processor?
tests.explanation: WP_HTML_Tag_Processor::MAX_BOOKMARKS is 10. WP_HTML_Processor overrides this to 100 to accommodate the more complex tree-aware parsing.

tests.prompt: WP_HTML_Processor overrides MAX_BOOKMARKS from its parent class. What is the new limit?
tests.explanation: WP_HTML_Processor sets MAX_BOOKMARKS to 100 (vs 10 in WP_HTML_Tag_Processor) because the tree construction algorithm needs more bookmarks to track the stack of open elements.

tests.prompt: WP_HTML_Processor::create_fragment() only supports one context element. Which one?
tests.explanation: create_fragment() only supports '<body>' as the context element (the default). Passing any other context returns null. This means all fragment parsing assumes the HTML is inside a body element.

tests.prompt:
When using WP_HTML_Processor::create_fragment() to parse '

text

', what does get_breadcrumbs() return when positioned on the STRONG tag?
tests.explanation: get_breadcrumbs() always includes the implicit outer elements. In a <body> fragment context, breadcrumbs start with array('HTML', 'BODY', ...) followed by the actual nesting path to the current node.

choices.text: It uses a regex-based cleanup pass over the HTML string
It creates a fragment parser and calls serialize() on it
It runs the Tag Processor in a loop, fixing each token

tests.explanation: normalize() is simply: static::create_fragment($html)->serialize(). It round-trips the HTML through the full HTML5 parser, producing normalized output with consistent quoting, lowercased names, and invalid UTF-8 replaced.

tests.prompt: In what letter case does WP_HTML_Tag_Processor::get_tag() return tag names?
tests.explanation: get_tag() always returns tag names in uppercase (e.g., 'DIV', 'SPAN', 'IMG'). This normalization matches how the HTML spec treats tag names as case-insensitive.
